title: NIS2 SCADA System Access Monitoring
exclusions: Does not determine legal applicability, complete regulator submissions, approve policy decisions, or replace counsel or competent-authority review. Does not certify functional safety of the controller—only visibility of access telemetry available to Splunk.
evidence: Scheduled saved search `UC-22.2.37`, dashboard panels on restricted NIS2 posture workbook, and archived exports in `index=audit_evidence` retained per your records policy.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing: SPL directly addresses Art.21(2)(a) requirements with specific, actionable monitoring.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(a) (Risk analysis and information-system security policies) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.37: NIS2 SCADA System Access Monitoring.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.37' running on WinEventLog:Security EventCode=4624/4648, cyberark:session, nis2_scada_maintenance_windows.csv, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: A contractor account starts a CyberArk session to asset HEX-PLANT-01 while `nis2_scada_assets.csv` lists only internal engineers for that asset_id; the saved search returns a row with nonzero sessions and actions including modify within a closed maintenance window.
controlTest.negativeScenario: An operator listed in `authorized_users` performs routine read-only polling during an open maintenance window with matching `window_start/window_end`; after roster and window enrichment the search returns zero notable rows for that session.

dataSources: `index=ot_access` with `sourcetype=scada:access_log` (operator logins, writes, engineering actions; extracted fields: `asset_id`, `access_user`, `session_id`, `action`, `result`). `index=pam` with `sourcetype=cyberark:session` (privileged sessions into OT; fields: `user`, `target_host`, `protocol`). Splunk Add-on for CyberArk ([Splunkbase 2891](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/2891)) and Splunk Enterprise Security ([Splunkbase 263](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/263)). KV/CSV lookup `nis2_scada_assets.csv` with columns `asset_id`, `asset_name`, `scada_type`, `criticality`, `authorized_users`. Optional CIM Authentication tagging for identity analytics.
app: Splunk Add-on for CyberArk (Splunkbase 2891), Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263)
spl:
(index=ot_access sourcetype=scada:access_log) OR (index=pam sourcetype=cyberark:session) earliest=-24h
| eval asset_id=coalesce(asset_id, plant_asset_id, target_host, dest_host, TargetAddress)
| eval access_user=coalesce(access_user, user, User, user_name)
| lookup nis2_scada_assets.csv asset_id OUTPUT asset_name, scada_type, criticality, authorized_users
| where isnotnull(scada_type) AND criticality IN ("high","critical")
| eval action_norm=lower(coalesce(action, activity, EventType, "login"))
| eval user_in_roster=if(match(lower(authorized_users), lower(access_user)), 1, 0)
| where user_in_roster=0 OR match(action_norm, "(write|modify|download|program|force)")
| lookup nis2_scada_maintenance_windows.csv asset_id OUTPUT start, end
| eval starttime=strptime(start,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S") | eval endtime=strptime(end,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval in_maint=if(_time>=starttime AND _time<=endtime,1,0)
| where in_maint=0 OR user_in_roster=0
| stats earliest(_time) as first_seen latest(_time) as last_seen values(action_norm) as actions dc(session_id) as sessions by asset_id asset_name scada_type access_user criticality
| sort - sessions
description: Detects logins, privileged sessions, and high-risk engineering actions against inventoried plant control assets when the actor is outside the approved user roster for that asset, or when the activity occurs outside an approved maintenance interval.
value: Turns scattered plant-access logs and vault sessions into defensible proof that only expected people touched critical control gear at expected times, so leadership can answer oversight questions without assembling spreadsheets after the fact.
implementation: Ingest `scada:access_log` into `index=ot_access` and CyberArk sessions into `index=pam` using Splunkbase 2891. Maintain `nis2_scada_assets.csv` and `nis2_scada_maintenance_windows.csv` under change control. Map fields `asset_id`, `access_user`, `action`, `session_id`. Schedule the saved search every 15 minutes over rolling 24 hours; route results to ES correlation or a compliance dashboard. Normalize identities toward the CIM Authentication model where possible for ES identity tables.
visualization: Table of asset_id, asset_name, access_user, actions, sessions; timeline of first_seen/last_seen; single value of distinct off-roster users; map or bar split by scada_type. Optional ES Incident Review drilldown when Splunkbase 263 is licensed.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication where nodename=Authentication by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src | sort - count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable for Authentication when ES is deployed; summary range 30 days minimum for roster analytics.
knownFalsePositives:
• **Break-glass CyberArk sessions** — Distinguish: ticketed emergency change with duty manager approval in ServiceNow. Suppress: time-bound rows in `nis2_scada_breakglass_exceptions.csv` keyed on `access_user`, `asset_id`, and expiry `_time`.
• **New hire or rotated contractor identity** — Distinguish: HR effective-dated roster in workday export matches `access_user` but lookup stale. Suppress: refresh `nis2_scada_assets.csv` same-day; short filter `where effective_date<=now()` after lookup enrichment.
• **Asset_id remapping after line shutdown** — Distinguish: old `asset_id` still appears on legacy HMI logs while CMDB shows merged tag. Suppress: maintain `nis2_scada_asset_aliases.csv` lookup mapping retired IDs to canonical `asset_id` before the roster check.
• **Vendor jump-server shared account** — Distinguish: single service account used by multiple named engineers; logs lack human name. Suppress: exception register column `shared_account_override` with mandatory ticket reference; join `cyberark:session` ClientHost before filtering.
• **Read-only monitoring poll mistaken as access** — Distinguish: `action` contains poll/heartbeat from IT monitoring; source subnet documented. Suppress: `where NOT cidrmatch("10.40.0.0/16", src_ip)` or dedicated allow list lookup `nis2_scada_monitoring_subnets.csv`.
equipmentModels: CyberArk Privileged Access Security (PAM for SCADA jump-host session logging)
Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928, change_request for SCADA maintenance windows)
Splunk Edge Hub (Modbus TCP/OPC-UA data from SCADA HMI/PLC network)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (Splunkbase 263, Authentication data model)
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• This UC implements NIS2 Art.21(2)(a) — risk analysis and information system security policies, and Art.21(2)(i) — human resources security, access control policies, and asset management, applied specifically to SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) systems. SCADA systems are high-value targets in critical infrastructure — unauthorised access can cause physical damage, environmental harm, or service disruption. This UC monitors authentication events and access patterns on SCADA systems to detect unauthorised or anomalous access.
• **What makes SCADA access monitoring different from IT access monitoring**: (a) SCADA systems often use shared accounts or local accounts rather than domain-integrated authentication. (b) Access is typically through specialised HMI (Human Machine Interface) workstations, not standard desktops. (c) Legitimate access follows predictable patterns — shift schedules, maintenance windows. (d) Any out-of-pattern access is potentially high-impact. (e) Many SCADA systems don't generate standard Windows/Linux authentication logs.
• **Data sources**:
  - `index=ot_auth sourcetype=scada:auth` — SCADA system authentication events. Sources vary by SCADA vendor:
    - Siemens WinCC: Windows Security event logs from WinCC server (Event ID 4624/4625)
    - Schneider ClearSCADA/EcoStruxure Geo SCADA: application-level audit log
    - GE iFIX: iFIX audit trail log
    - AVEVA (Wonderware) InTouch/System Platform: security audit
    - OSIsoft PI: PI System audit database
    Fields (normalised): `user` (username), `action` (login, logout, failed_login), `source_ip` (where the login originated), `target_system` (the SCADA server/HMI hostname), `auth_method` (local, domain, smartcard, badge).
  - `index=pam sourcetype=cyberark:session` — CyberArk PAM sessions through SCADA jump-hosts (same as UC-22.2.50 but filtered to OT zones).
  - `nis2_scada_access_policy.csv` — the access control matrix. Columns: `user` (authorised username), `allowed_systems` (comma-separated SCADA systems the user can access), `allowed_hours` (shift pattern, e.g., "06-18" for day shift, "18-06" for night shift, "00-24" for 24/7), `role` (operator, engineer, administrator, vendor), `change_required` (yes/no — whether a change ticket is required before access).
  - `nis2_scada_maintenance_windows.csv` — scheduled maintenance windows. Columns: `target_system`, `start_time`, `end_time`, `change_ticket`, `description`.
• **Shared account challenge**: many SCADA systems use shared operator accounts (e.g., "OPERATOR1", "SHIFT_A"). This makes individual attribution impossible from SCADA logs alone. Fix: use CyberArk PAM as the authentication layer — the CyberArk session ties the individual user's identity to the shared account session.
• **RBAC**: role `nis2_ot_security` with `srchIndexesAllowed = ot_auth, pam, itsm, audit_evidence`. SCADA access data reveals the operational technology architecture — extremely sensitive.
• **Index retention**: `index=ot_auth` ≥ 2 years (access audit trail for incident investigation). `index=audit_evidence` ≥ 7 years.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
**SCADA authentication events:**
The data collection method depends on the SCADA vendor:
- **Siemens WinCC on Windows**: install a Splunk Universal Forwarder on the WinCC server. Configure `inputs.conf` to monitor Windows Security events:
```ini
[WinEventLog://Security]
disabled = 0
index = ot_auth
evt_resolve_ad_obj = 1
```
Apply `props.conf` field aliasing to map Windows event fields to the normalised SCADA schema.

- **AVEVA/Wonderware InTouch**: export the InTouch security audit log to a file share. Monitor with Splunk UF:
```ini
[monitor://C:\InTouch\SecurityAudit\*.log]
sourcetype = scada:auth
index = ot_auth
```

- **OSIsoft PI**: use the PI System audit trail API or PI SQL Data Access to query audit events. POST to HEC.

Verify: `index=ot_auth sourcetype=scada:auth earliest=-7d | stats count dc(user) as users dc(target_system) as systems values(action) as actions`.

**SCADA access policy lookup:**
Place `nis2_scada_access_policy.csv` in `$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/TA-nis2-ot/lookups/`. Example:
```csv
user,allowed_systems,allowed_hours,role,change_required
jsmith,SCADA-HMI-01;SCADA-HMI-02,06-18,operator,no
eng-knutsen,SCADA-ENG-01;SCADA-HMI-01;SCADA-HMI-02;SCADA-PLC-GW,00-24,engineer,yes
vendor-abb,SCADA-PLC-GW,08-17,vendor,yes
```

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Save as `UC-22.2.37 NIS2 SCADA Access Monitoring`.

Understanding this SPL — design decisions:

• **Three detection conditions**:
  - **Unauthorised user**: `lookup nis2_scada_access_policy.csv user OUTPUT allowed_systems | where isnull(allowed_systems)` identifies users not in the access control matrix. Any login by an unknown user to a SCADA system is a critical finding.
  - **Out-of-hours access**: `eval hour=strftime(_time,"%H") | where (hour < tonumber(mvindex(split(allowed_hours,"-"),0)) OR hour >= tonumber(mvindex(split(allowed_hours,"-"),1)))` identifies logins outside the user's authorised shift window. An operator with `allowed_hours=06-18` logging in at 02:00 is anomalous.
  - **Access without change ticket**: for users with `change_required=yes` (engineers and vendors), the SPL checks for a corresponding approved change ticket in ServiceNow within ±2 hours of the access event. Access without a ticket is a violation of the change management policy.

• **Failed login detection**: `action=failed_login` events are aggregated. More than 5 failed logins in 1 hour to a SCADA system triggers a brute-force alert. SCADA systems often lack account lockout policies (to ensure availability), making brute-force attacks viable.

• **CyberArk correlation**: for environments using CyberArk as the jump-host, the SPL joins PAM session data with SCADA auth events by time window and target system. This ties the individual identity (CyberArk) to the SCADA session (which may use a shared account).

Schedule: cron `*/5 * * * *` (every 5 minutes). SCADA access anomalies require near-real-time detection — the impact of unauthorised SCADA access can be physical and immediate. Trigger: `Number of results > 0`.

Alert actions: (1) For unauthorised users: PagerDuty page to the OT SOC and plant shift supervisor — immediate response required. (2) For out-of-hours access: email to the OT security manager. (3) For change ticket violations: email to the SCADA system owner. (4) For brute-force attempts: PagerDuty page to the SOC. (5) Write to `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=nis2:scada_access:evidence`.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) **Unauthorised user detection**: have a test user (not in the access policy CSV) attempt a login to a SCADA system. Verify the UC detects the unknown user within 5 minutes.

(b) **Out-of-hours detection**: have an authorised operator log in outside their shift window. Verify the UC detects the out-of-hours access.

(c) **Change ticket validation**: have an engineer access a SCADA system without first creating a ServiceNow change ticket. Verify the UC flags the access as `change_required` violation.

(d) **Failed login threshold**: generate 6 failed logins to a SCADA HMI within 1 hour. Verify the brute-force alert triggers.

(e) **CyberArk attribution**: if using CyberArk, have a user connect through the jump-host to a SCADA system. Verify the UC correlates the CyberArk session with the SCADA auth event and attributes the correct individual user.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard (recommended name: `NIS2 SCADA Access`):
• **Row 1 — Access posture**: Single-value `Authorised users`. Single-value `Unknown user logins (24h)` (red ≥ 1). Single-value `Out-of-hours access (24h)` (amber ≥ 1). Single-value `Failed login attempts (24h)`.
• **Row 2 — Access timeline**: `index=ot_auth sourcetype=scada:auth earliest=-24h | timechart span=1h count by user limit=10`. Line chart showing who accessed SCADA systems and when. Overlay shift boundaries.
• **Row 3 — System access matrix**: table of `user` vs `target_system` with access counts. Highlight cells where the user is not authorised for that system.
• **Row 4 — Failed login detail**: table of `_time`, `user`, `source_ip`, `target_system`, `action` for all `failed_login` events.

Runbook (owner: OT Security Manager / Plant Manager):
1. Every 5 minutes (automated): investigate unauthorised user alerts immediately. Verify: is the user a new hire who hasn't been added to the access policy CSV? Or is this a genuine security incident? If the latter, initiate the OT incident response procedure.
2. Daily: review out-of-hours access. Correlate with the shift schedule and maintenance calendar. If legitimate, document. If unexplained, investigate.
3. Weekly: review failed login trends. Persistent failures on specific SCADA systems may indicate targeted attacks.
4. Monthly: update the `nis2_scada_access_policy.csv` for personnel changes (new hires, terminations, role changes).

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **SCADA systems don't generate authentication logs.** Older SCADA systems (pre-2010) may not have audit logging capabilities. Fix: implement network-level monitoring: use a network tap or SPAN port to capture traffic to SCADA systems. Detect access by monitoring SSH/RDP/VNC connections to SCADA IPs.

• **Shared account makes `user` field meaningless.** The SCADA log shows "OPERATOR1" for all operator logins. Fix: require all SCADA access to pass through CyberArk. The CyberArk session log provides the individual identity. Map `session_start/end` from CyberArk to SCADA auth `_time` to attribute the session.

• **Shift schedule changes not reflected in CSV.** Operators swap shifts but the CSV isn't updated. Fix: integrate with the workforce management system (e.g., Kronos, SAP HCM) to pull current shift assignments. Or accept a higher false positive rate for out-of-hours access and tune the alert to only fire for critical systems.

• **Vendor remote access uses VPN, not jump-host.** Some vendors connect via VPN directly to the SCADA network without passing through CyberArk. Fix: mandate all vendor SCADA access through CyberArk (policy enforcement). Until then, monitor VPN logs for vendor connections: `index=vpn src_user=vendor-* | lookup nis2_scada_access_policy.csv user AS src_user OUTPUT allowed_systems`.

• **Time synchronisation issues between SCADA and Splunk.** SCADA systems may not use NTP, causing time drift. Fix: verify SCADA system time with: `index=ot_auth sourcetype=scada:auth | eval drift_sec=abs(_indextime-_time) | stats avg(drift_sec) max(drift_sec) by target_system`. If drift > 300 seconds, configure NTP on the SCADA server.
grandmaExplanation: We watch who connects to the computers that run important plants and factories. When someone shows up who should not be there, or they poke around outside an agreed maintenance window, we highlight it so leaders can ask good questions before harm spreads.
